quote: 1. The stock doesn't know you own it. You feel emotional about a stock, but the stock doesn't feel anything about you.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 2. In this business, if you're good, you're right six times out of ten. You're never going to be right nine times out of ten.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 3. Far more money has been lost by investors preparing for corrections, or trying to anticipate corrections, than has been lost in corrections themselves.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 4. Know what you own, and know why you own it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 5. Go for a business that any idiot can run — because eventually, any idiot probably is going to run it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 6. Long shots almost never pay off.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 7. You should be able to explain why you own a stock in such simple terms that a fifth grader could understand it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 8. Numbers are nice, but the basic story line is what matters.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 9. Owning stocks is like having children — don't get involved with more than you can handle.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 10. The best stock to buy may be the one you already own.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 11. Investing without research is like playing stud poker and never looking at the cards.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 12. Time is on your side when you own shares of superior companies.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 13. In the long run, it's not just how much money you make that will determine your future prosperity. It's how much of that money you put to work by saving it and investing it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 14. Everyone has the brainpower to make money in stocks. Not everyone has the stomach.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 15. Understanding the story of a stock you own or intend to buy is as basic as understanding why your kid needs a shot.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 16. The person that turns over the most rocks wins the game.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 17. Absent a lot of surprises, stocks are relatively predictable over twenty years. As to whether they're going to be higher or lower in two or three years, you might as well flip a coin to decide.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 18. If you like the store, chances are you'll love the stock.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 19. Never invest in any idea you can't illustrate with a crayon.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 20. Selling your winners and holding your losers is like cutting the flowers and watering the weeds.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 21. Gentlemen who prefer bonds don't know what they're missing.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 22. When you sell in desperation, you always sell cheap.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 23. Behind every stock is a company. Find out what it's doing.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 24. All you need for a lifetime of successful investing is a few big winners, and the pluses from those will overwhelm the minuses from the stocks that don't work out.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 25. Every once in a while, the market does something so stupid it takes your breath away.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 26. There's no point in getting insight into a company if you don't stick around long enough to profit from it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 27. In the long run, a portfolio of well-chosen stocks and/or equity mutual funds will always outperform a portfolio of bonds or a money-market account.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 28. You can't see the future through a rearview mirror.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 29. Just because Warren Buffett is buying a stock doesn't mean you should buy it too.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 30. There is always something to worry about. Avoid weekend thinking and ignore the latest dire predictions of newsletter writers.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 31. A decline in the stock market is as routine as a January blizzard in Colorado.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 32. If you can't convince yourself 'When I'm down 25 percent, I'm a buyer' and banish forever the fatal thought 'When I'm down 25 percent, I'm a seller,' then you'll never make a decent profit in stocks.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 33. All the math you need in the stock market you get in the fourth grade.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 34. Averaging down in a company that has fundamental problems is like adding water to a watered-down drink.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 35. Companies that have no debt can't go bankrupt.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 36. Just avoid the losers and the fear will take care of itself.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 37. Big companies have small moves, small companies have big moves.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 38. If a company uses a technology you don't understand, what it accomplishes, and how it can serve customers, don't invest in it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 39. Invest in the company with the fewest color photographs in the annual report.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 40. I made most of my money out of the third and fourth year I owned something, not the first year.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 41. Nobody can predict interest rates, the future direction of the economy, or the stock market. Dismiss all such forecasts and concentrate on what's actually happening to the companies in which you've invested.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 42. There's no shame in losing money on a stock. Everybody does it. What is shameful is holding on to a stock, or worse, buying more of it, when the fundamentals are deteriorating.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 43. Time is your friend, impulse is your enemy.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 44. Corporations, like people, change their names for one of two reasons: either they've gotten married, or they've been involved in some fiasco they'd like to forget.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 45. The extra assurance of 'inside' information is worth nothing to the buyer. In fact, it may cost him everything. He may as well flip a coin.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 46. In stocks, as in romance, ease of divorce is not a sound basis for commitment.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 47. Never invest in any company without understanding its finances. The biggest losses in stocks come from companies with poor balance sheets.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 48. If you spend more than 13 minutes analyzing economic and market forecasts, you've wasted 10 minutes.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 49. The real key to making money in stocks is not to get scared out of them.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 50. Charts are great for predicting the past.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 51. As long as you stick with noncyclicals, the panic and the crash can't hurt you unless you panic along with everyone else and sell at the bottom.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 52. Look for small companies that are already profitable and have proven that their concept can be replicated.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 53. Better than boring alone is a stock that's boring and disgusting at the same time. Something that makes people shrug, retch or turn away in disgust is ideal.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 54. Gold, much more so than any other commodity, is about sentiment and psychology.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 55. My advice for the next decade: Keep on the lookout for tomorrow's big baggers. You're likely to find one.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 56. These are among my favorite investments: small aggressive new enterprises that grow at 20 to 25 percent a year. If you choose wisely, this is the land of the 10- to 40-baggers, and even the 200-baggers.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 57. A price drop in a good stock is only a tragedy if you sell at that price and never buy more.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 58. Investing in stocks is an art, not a science, and people who've been trained to rigidly quantify everything have a big disadvantage.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 59. You can lose money in a very short time, but it takes a long time to make money.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 60. In my business a four bagger is nice, but a ten bagger is the fiscal equivalent of two home runs and a double.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 61. Frequently, there is no correlation between the success of a company's operations and the success of its stock over a few months or even years, but eventually, the correlation is 100 percent.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 62. Buy your straw hats in winter.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 63. The wonderful thing about the free market system is that eventually, brilliant ideas do get funded, and dumb ideas usually don't.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 64. Stop listening to professionals! Twenty years in this business convinces me that any normal person using the customary three percent of the brain can pick stocks just as well, if not better, than the average Wall Street expert.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 65. I'm always on the lookout for great companies in lousy industries.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 66. Find a company that no analyst has ever visited, or that no analyst would admit to knowing about, and you've got a double winner.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 67. The trick is not to learn to trust your gut feelings, but rather to discipline yourself to ignore them. Stand by your stocks as long as the fundamental story of the company hasn't changed.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 68. An important key to investing is to remember that stocks are not lottery tickets.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 69. In 99 cases out of 100, a house will be a money-maker.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 70. If you're lucky enough to have one golden egg in your portfolio, it may not matter if you have a couple of rotten ones in there with it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 71. Although it's easy to forget sometimes, a share is not a lottery ticket. It's part ownership of a business.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 72. Everyone has the brainpower to follow the stock market. If you made it through fifth-grade math, you can do it.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 73. People worry about the riskiness of stocks, but bonds can be just as risky.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 74. Ultimately it is not the stock market nor even the companies themselves that determine an investor's fate. It is the investor.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 75. Investing is fun and exciting, but dangerous if you don't do any work.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 76. The key organ here isn't the brain, it's the stomach.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 77. In business, competition is never as healthy as total domination.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 78. If you can follow only one bit of data, follow the earnings — assuming the company in question has earnings. I subscribe to the crusty notion that sooner or later earnings make or break an investment in equities. What the stock price does today, tomorrow, or next week is only a distraction.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 79. Your investor's edge is not something you get from Wall Street experts. It's something you already have. You can outperform the experts if you use your edge by investing in companies or industries you already understand.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 80. Remember, things are never clear until it's too late.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 81. While catching up on the news is merely depressing to the citizen who has no stocks, it is a dangerous habit for the investor.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 82. The more cash that builds up in the treasury, the greater the pressure to piss it away.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 83. If you can't find any companies that you think are attractive, put your money in the bank until you discover some.
speaker: Peter Lynch

quote: 84. I can't imagine anything that's useful to know that the amateur investor can't find out.
speaker: Peter Lynch
